title: Food Is Fuel, Sleep Builds You Strong
pillar_name: Physical Literacy, Martial Arts & Human Performance

required.title: [Read Aloud] Eat Your Colors
required.author: various authors or similar nutrition-for-kids books

required.title: [Equipment] A water bottle the student carries daily

supplementary.title: [Visual] A food chart showing protein sources

competence_markers.description: The student can identify real food versus processed food on a plate and in a grocery store
The student can name at least five good protein sources
The student includes protein at most meals without prompting
The student drinks water consistently throughout the day and around training
The student can explain, in their own words, why sleep matters for getting stronger
The student sleeps 10+ hours per night on a consistent schedule
The student eats enough to support training — no chronic fatigue, consistent energy, steady growth

cross_connections.description: Consistent eating, hydration, and sleep habits are daily discipline in action. The student who does these without being reminded is demonstrating IC2 competence.
Kitchen time and food identification connect directly. The student who trains hard should also begin learning to prepare food.
Physical training without proper nutrition produces limited results. These packets reinforce each other.
The basic biology of protein synthesis, growth hormone, and energy systems connects to science education as it develops.

content.why_this_matters:
Your body is like a building that is always being built. Every single day, your body is tearing down old parts and building new ones — new muscle, new bone, new skin, new everything. But here is the question: what is it building with?

The answer is food. The food you eat becomes your body. That is not a figure of speech. The protein in the chicken you eat tonight will become the muscle in your arm tomorrow. The calcium in the milk you drink will become the bone in your leg. The food you eat is literally the construction material your body uses to build itself.

Now think about this: if you build a house out of strong bricks and good timber, you get a strong house. If you build a house out of cardboard and tape, you get a weak house. Your body works the same way. If you eat real food — meat, vegetables, fruit, eggs, fish — your body builds strong muscle, hard bones, and a brain that works well. If you eat mostly candy, chips, and sugary drinks, your body builds with weak materials. It still builds — but what it builds is not as good.

The other thing your body needs to build itself is sleep. When you are sleeping, your body is doing its most important construction work. Growth hormone — the chemical that makes you taller and stronger — is released mostly during deep sleep. Your muscles repair from training during sleep. Your brain organizes everything you learned that day during sleep. If you train hard and eat well but do not sleep enough, you are tearing your body down without giving it time to build back up.

This packet teaches you the basics: eat real food, eat enough of it, drink water, and sleep. These sound simple. They are simple. But most people do not do them, and the consequences show in their bodies, their energy, and their performance.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **The understanding that food is construction material** — what you eat becomes your body
2. **The ability to identify real food versus processed food** — recognizing what came from an animal, a plant, or a factory
3. **Protein awareness** — understanding that muscles need protein to grow and recover
4. **The water habit** — drinking water consistently, especially around training
5. **The connection between sleep and physical development** — understanding that you grow and get stronger while you sleep
6. **The habit of eating enough** — training children need fuel, and under-eating is a real problem
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Real Food Versus Processed Food

There is a simple test you can use to figure out whether something is real food: could this thing exist without a factory?

An apple could exist without a factory. A chicken could exist without a factory. An egg, a carrot, a fish, a glass of milk — all of these exist in nature. They are real food.

A bag of chips could not exist without a factory. A candy bar could not exist without a factory. A bottle of soda could not exist without a factory. These are processed foods — things that were made in a factory using ingredients that have been changed, combined, and packaged to taste a certain way.

Here is the important part: this is not about being good or bad. Nobody is saying you can never have a cookie. What we are saying is that you should understand the difference. Real food — food that came from an animal or a plant — is what your body uses to build itself. Processed food gives your body energy (usually too much energy, in the form of sugar) but not much building material. If you eat mostly real food, your body has what it needs. If you eat mostly processed food, your body is working with poor materials.

**The plate test:** Look at your plate at any meal. Can you identify where each thing came from? "That chicken came from a chicken. Those carrots came from the ground. That rice came from a rice plant." If most of your plate is real food you can trace to an animal or a plant, you are eating well. If most of your plate is things that came in a package from a factory, there is room to improve.

### Part 2: Protein — The Building Block of Muscle

Of all the things your body needs from food, protein is the most important for someone who is training. Here is why:

When you train — when you run, climb, wrestle, do push-ups, carry heavy things — you are actually creating tiny amounts of damage in your muscles. This is normal and good. Your body repairs that damage and makes the muscle a little bit stronger than it was before. That is how you get stronger: damage, repair, stronger. Damage, repair, stronger.

But what does your body use to repair the muscle? Protein. Protein is the building material for muscle. Without enough protein, your body cannot repair the damage from training, and you do not get stronger. You just stay sore.

**Where protein comes from:**
- Meat — chicken, beef, pork, turkey, lamb. Meat is the richest source of protein.
- Fish — salmon, tuna, cod, shrimp. Fish is excellent protein and also gives you healthy fats.
- Eggs — one of the best and cheapest sources of protein. The whole egg, not just the white.
- Dairy — milk, cheese, yogurt. Full-fat is better than low-fat for a growing, training child.
- Beans and lentils — a plant source of protein. Not as complete as animal sources, but useful.

**The simple rule:** Every meal should have a protein source. Not just dinner — breakfast and lunch too. Eggs at breakfast. Chicken or tuna at lunch. Meat or fish at dinner. Milk or cheese as snacks. If every meal has protein, your body has the building material it needs to grow and recover from training.

### Part 3: Water — The Simplest Thing Most People Get Wrong

Your body is mostly water. Your muscles are mostly water. Your blood is mostly water. When you train, you sweat, and when you sweat, you lose water. If you lose too much water without replacing it, your body stops working well. Your muscles cramp. Your brain gets foggy. Your energy disappears.

The solution is simple: drink water. Not soda. Not juice (which is mostly sugar). Water.

**When to drink:**
- When you wake up — your body has gone all night without water
- Before training — make sure you start hydrated
- During training — small sips, not huge gulps
- After training — replace what you lost
- Throughout the day — carry your water bottle everywhere

**How to know if you are drinking enough:** Look at your urine. If it is pale yellow or clear, you are well hydrated. If it is dark yellow, you need to drink more. This is not a delicate topic — it is the simplest hydration test that exists, and it works.

**A note about thirst:** By the time you feel thirsty, you are already somewhat dehydrated. Thirst is a lagging indicator — it tells you that you needed water a while ago. Do not wait until you are thirsty. Drink regularly throughout the day.

### Part 4: Sleep — When the Real Building Happens

Here is something that might surprise you: you do not get stronger when you train. You get stronger when you sleep.

Training creates the signal — it tells your body "we need to be stronger, we need to be faster, we need more muscle." But the actual building happens during sleep. Growth hormone, the chemical that makes your muscles grow and your bones get stronger, is released primarily during deep sleep. If you do not get enough deep sleep, you do not get enough growth hormone, and the training signal goes unanswered.

This means that a child who trains hard and sleeps well will get stronger, faster, and bigger. A child who trains hard and sleeps poorly will just be tired and sore. Training without adequate sleep is like ordering construction materials but never letting the builders work.

**How much sleep you need:** At your age, 10–12 hours per night. Not 8. Not "whenever I feel tired." Your body needs 10–12 hours to complete all the repair and growth work that training demands. This is not negotiable. It is biology.

**How to sleep well:**
- Go to bed at the same time every night. Your body has an internal clock, and it works best when the schedule is consistent.
- Make your room dark. Light tells your brain to wake up. Darkness tells it to sleep. Even small lights — a phone screen, a night light, a glowing clock — can disrupt sleep quality.
- No screens for at least 30 minutes before bed. Screens emit blue light that tells your brain it is daytime. Reading a book or talking with your family before bed is better than watching a screen.
- Your room should be cool. A slightly cool room produces better sleep than a warm one.

### Part 5: Eating Enough — A Problem Most People Do Not Expect

Here is a problem that happens more often than you might think: training children do not eat enough.

When you are training hard — running, wrestling, doing push-ups, climbing, carrying things — your body is burning a lot of energy. That energy comes from food. If you do not eat enough food, your body does not have the energy to train well, and it does not have the building material to recover and grow.

Skipping meals on training days is a bad idea. Eating only a little because you are not hungry right after training is a missed opportunity — your body is ready to rebuild, and it needs fuel to do it. Eating only snack foods instead of real meals leaves you without enough protein and nutrients.

**The simple guidance:** Eat real food at every meal. Include protein at every meal. Do not skip meals, especially on training days. If you are training hard and feeling tired, sluggish, or constantly sore, you may not be eating enough. More food — real food — is often the answer.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Food Recognition
**Developing well:** The student can look at a plate and identify what came from animals, plants, and factories. They do this naturally, without prompting. They show interest in where food comes from.

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot distinguish real food from processed food, or has no interest in the question.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you played the food identification game regularly? At meals, in grocery stores, at restaurants? This is a recognition skill built through repetition.

### 2. Protein Habit
**Developing well:** The student expects protein at every meal. They notice when it is missing ("there's no protein on this plate"). They voluntarily choose protein-rich foods.

**Developing slowly:** The student defaults to carbohydrate-heavy meals (pasta, bread, cereal) and resists protein foods.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is protein available and appealing? Some children resist meat but will eat eggs, cheese, or yogurt. Find the protein sources the student enjoys and make them consistently available.

### 3. Hydration
**Developing well:** The student carries their water bottle and drinks throughout the day without reminders. They drink water before and after training.

**Developing slowly:** The student forgets the water bottle, prefers juice or soda, and only drinks when reminded.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the water bottle consistently available? Is the student drinking other beverages (juice, soda) that reduce water consumption? Replace sugary drinks with water as the default. The habit forms through environmental control.

### 4. Sleep Quality and Quantity
**Developing well:** The student sleeps 10+ hours consistently, follows the bedtime routine without resistance, and wakes rested and energized.

**Developing slowly:** The student resists bedtime, has inconsistent sleep schedules, wakes tired, or has difficulty falling asleep.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the bedroom dark and cool? Is screen time eliminated before bed? Is the bedtime consistent? Is the child consuming caffeine (including in soda or chocolate) in the afternoon? Are there stressors (anxiety, overstimulation) that interfere with sleep onset?
